item.title: I built my own smart TV setup with a monitor and a cheap device, and it turned out better than I expected
item.itunes:title: I built my own smart TV setup with a monitor and a cheap device, and it turned out better than I expected
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
A smart TV is generally the default choice for anyone who wants a screen for casual gaming, streaming, and everything in between. Some of the upper-end options make things easier by pairing solid speakers with a good display, but the day-to-day experience doesn't tend to be dramatically different from that of a mid-range option.

Sometimes, a budget-friendly setup can also become a practical solution for a daily entertainment fix, and that became apparent when I paired my monitor with a cheap smart TV stick. This setup covered nearly everything I was looking for, and was more flexible and faster than what I have experienced on most affordable TVs.

Smart TVs are convenient, but they also require compromises

An all-in-one approach isn't always the smart option

There's a good reason why smart TVs have become the default choice. They bring all-in-one convenience, and for most people, living room entertainment in a single package is hard to argue against. However, this convenience comes with some trade-offs, some of which can't just be easily ignored. While upper-end models come with a good panel, strong speakers, and features like high refresh rates, the core experience tends to be much the same.

For example, the software experience may feel less responsive than it should be. The wait time between powering on the TV and content playing is another pain point. These aren't always a dealbreaker, but they do degrade the overall fluidity of the experience.

Long-term support is another weak point. While the displays of smart TVs can stay perfectly fine for years, the software tends to age faster. Updates slow down, and apps lose support as manufacturers shift their attention to newer models.

This simple trick fixed my laggy smart TV

If your smart TV's operating system is stuttering, clearing its cache is often a quick way to speed it up.

2
By  Patrick O'Rourke

The DIY approach with a cheap dongle is more flexible

A monitor with an affordable streaming stick turned out to be more than enough

You wouldn't be re-inventing the smart TV concept with the DIY approach. Instead, the core idea is to turn your already available monitor into a secondary entertainment screen without putting a dent in your wallet. While this means the all-in-one convenience isn't there anymore, I was genuinely surprised at how complete the experience felt.

I paired my gaming monitor with a 2020 Fire TV Stick 3rd Gen, which was basically lying around in one of my drawers. While it's old, it handled the smart side of things pretty well. Day-to-day apps started normally, and the lack of support for anything beyond 1080p 60Hz didn't bother me much either.

If I want higher-resolution output, Dolby Vision support, or a faster interface later, I can simply replace the streaming stick rather than the entire screen. Moreover, the smart TV stick dongle can stay connected to a secondary HDMI port on my monitor, and I can easily switch back to the primary input when I need to display my PC.

My DIY setup's performance isn't perfect, but it's good enough

Especially when compared to other budget-friendly smart TVs

While the Fire TV Stick 3rd Gen isn't the fastest smart TV stick out there, it still holds its own for day-to-day entertainment. To be precise, I wasn't expecting the setup to feel smoother than budget TVs, but that's exactly what happened.

It doesn't take ages for the home interface to load after startup. General navigation is manageable, and with 5GHz Wi-Fi support, the network speed is more than good enough for 1080p streaming. Of course, there were occasional delays while launching heavier apps, and I wouldn't recommend this TV stick for native Android gaming.

Subscribe to the newsletter for smart TV & DIY tips

Get smarter setups by subscribing to the newsletter for clear coverage of DIY smart-TV solutions, streaming-stick options, monitor repurposing, and trade-offs. Device comparisons, setup walkthroughs, and practical buying guidance to inform your next upgra
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

If performance becomes a big issue in the future, I can easily swap out the Fire TV Stick for something like the Fire TV Stick 4K Max or the Google TV Streamer 4K. For traditional smart TVs, on the other hand, there are no simple hardware upgrades.

I replaced my Nvidia Shield with a Chromecast, and the one thing I miss isn't what you'd expect

Here's how AI upscaling enhances older TV shows and YouTube videos, making them look more modern and crisp on the Nvidia Shield.

14
By  Dhruv Bhutani

Smart TVs still make sense for many people

This DIY setup works only when flexibility is the main priority

Even though DIY smart TV setups are affordable and flexible, they aren't a universal replacement. For instance, it would be a bit challenging to get the classic couch-based entertainment experience with such a setup. Another notable factor is that most large monitors max out at 57-inches. So, for those who might prefer a large-screen experience, a TV 75-inch or larger would make more sense.

However, for anyone looking to expand the functionality of an existing monitor or repurpose it, a cheap device like the Fire TV Stick HD is the practical option. It may not fully replace a traditional TV, but for a secondary entertainment setup where flexibility matters more than screen size, the DIY approach makes a lot of sense.

Fire TV Stick HD (2026)
$22 $35 Save $13
$22 at Amazon

By  Dhruv Bhutani

Smart TVs still make sense for many people

This DIY setup works only when flexibility is the main priority

Even though DIY smart TV setups are affordable and flexible, they aren't a universal replacement. For instance, it would be a bit challenging to get the classic couch-based entertainment experience with such a setup. Another notable factor is that most large monitors max out at 57-inches. So, for those who might prefer a large-screen experience, a TV 75-inch or larger would make more sense.

However, for anyone looking to expand the functionality of an existing monitor or repurpose it, a cheap device like the Fire TV Stick HD is the practical option. It may not fully replace a traditional TV, but for a secondary entertainment setup where flexibility matters more than screen size, the DIY approach makes a lot of sense.
